Amr receives $\frac{1}{3}$ of the total number of coins that Bai, Cindy and Derek receive. Bai receives $\frac{1}{5}$ of the total number of coins that Amr, Cindy and Derek receive. Cindy receives $\frac{1}{7}$ of the total number of coins that Amr, Bai and Derek receive. If $N < 100$, what is the largest possible value of $N$?
solution: Let the numbers of coins received by Amr, Bai, Cindy, and Derek be $A,B,C,D$. The conditions give $$A=\frac13(B+C+D),\qquad B=\frac15(A+C+D),\qquad C=\frac17(A+B+D).$$ These are equivalent to $$3A=B+C+D,\qquad 5B=A+C+D,\qquad 7C=A+B+D.$$ Subtracting the first equation from the second gives $B=\frac23A$, and subtracting the first from the third gives $C=\frac12A$. Substituting into $3A=B+C+D$ gives $D=\frac{11}{6}A$. Therefore $$N=A+B+C+D=A+\frac23A+\frac12A+\frac{11}{6}A=4A,$$ so $N$ must be a multiple of $4$. The largest multiple of $4$ below $100$ is $96$, and it works with $(A,B,C,D)=(24,16,12,44)$.
credit: 2025 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2025 Problem 24
statement: A circle has area $A$, a regular octagon has area $\frac{1}{2}A$, and a regular dodecagon has area $\frac{1}{2}A$. The circle has radius $3000$. The distance from the centre of the octagon to each of its vertices is $x$, as shown. Also, the distance from the centre of the dodecagon to each of its vertices is $y$. What is the integer closest to $x-y$? (A regular polygon is a polygon all of whose side lengths are equal and all of whose interior angles are equal. An octagon has $8$ sides and a dodecagon has $12$ sides.)

statementImages.alt: Official regular-polygon diagram for Cayley 2025 Problem 24.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: A regular $n$-gon with circumradius $R$ has area $\frac{n}{2}R^2\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{n}\right)$. For the octagon, $$\frac{8}{2}x^2\sin45^\circ=4x^2\cdot\frac{\sqrt2}{2}=2\sqrt2\,x^2.$$ For the dodecagon, $$\frac{12}{2}y^2\sin30^\circ=6y^2\cdot\frac12=3y^2.$$ The circle has area $A=\pi(3000)^2$, so each polygon has area $\frac12A=2\pi(1500)^2$. Thus $$2\sqrt2\,x^2=2\pi(1500)^2,\qquad 3y^2=2\pi(1500)^2.$$ Hence $$x=1500\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{\sqrt2}}\approx2235.6752,\qquad y=1500\sqrt{\frac{2\pi}{3}}\approx2170.8038.$$ Therefore $x-y\approx64.87$, so the nearest integer is $65$.
credit: 2025 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2025 Problem 25
statement: Rita is colouring a picture of a flower. She has already coloured the centre and the stem of the flower, as shown. Next, she will colour each of the six petals with exactly one of the colours: red, orange, yellow, and blue. No two neighbouring petals can be the same colour and not all four colours need to be used. There are $N$ different-looking ways in which Rita can colour the petals. What are the rightmost two digits of $N$?

statementImages.alt: Official six-petal flower diagram for Cayley 2025 Problem 25.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Fix the top petal to be red and count valid colourings. If red is used on $3$ petals, there are $27$ colourings. If red is used on $2$ petals, there are $108$ colourings. If red is used on only the top petal, then the other five petals can be coloured in $3\times2\times2\times2\times2=48$ ways. So with the top petal fixed red, there are $$27+108+48=183$$ valid colourings. The top petal could originally have been any of the $4$ colours, so $$N=4\times183=732.$$ The rightmost two digits are $32$.
credit: 2025 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 1
statement: Evaluate $13+15+17$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 2
statement: One million is equal to which power of $10$?
solution: One million is $1{,}000{,}000$, which is $10^6$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 3
statement: If $4x+1=13$, what is the value of $12x+3$?
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 4
statement: On the number line from $0$ to $6$, the interval is divided into $12$ equal parts. Points $M$ and $N$ are marked as shown. Find $N-M$.

statementImages.alt: Number line from 0 to 6 divided into 12 equal parts with points M and N marked.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Each small interval has length $\frac{6}{12}=0.5$. Point $M$ is $2$ intervals to the right of $0$, so $M=1.0$. Point $N$ is $3$ intervals to the left of $6$, so $N=4.5$. Thus $N-M=4.5-1.0=3.5$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 5
statement: Carley ran on May $18$, did not run on May $19$, and kept alternating between running and not running. Between May $18$ and May $25$ inclusive, on how many days did she run?
solution: She ran on May $18,20,22,$ and $24$, so she ran on $4$ days.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 6
statement: Jin has $40$ cupcakes. Exactly $75\%$ are chocolate and the rest are vanilla. How many more vanilla cupcakes must Jin bake so that half are vanilla and half are chocolate?
solution: Jin has $30$ chocolate and $10$ vanilla cupcakes. To make the counts equal, Jin must add $20$ more vanilla cupcakes.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 7
statement: Which of the following integers leaves the same remainder when divided by $3$ as when divided by $4$?
solution: Check the remainders. Only $13$ gives remainder $1$ when divided by $3$ and also remainder $1$ when divided by $4$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 8
statement: Point $Q(4,2)$ is shifted left $5$ units and then down $6$ units to point $R$. What are the coordinates of $R$?
solution: Shifting left $5$ gives $(-1,2)$. Shifting down $6$ then gives $(-1,-4)$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 9
statement: A rectangular prism has integer edge lengths. Three face areas are $12\text{ cm}^2$, $20\text{ cm}^2$, and $15\text{ cm}^2$. Find its volume.
solution: If the side lengths are $\ell,w,h$, then the face areas are $\ell w$, $\ell h$, and $wh$. Their product is $$(\ell w)(\ell h)(wh)=(\ell wh)^2=12\cdot 20\cdot 15=3600.$$ So the volume is $$\ell wh=\sqrt{3600}=60.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 10
statement: Three cards lie face up showing $3$, $7$, and $8$. Jen flips the $3$, then the $7$, then the $8$. Each time, the mean of the three visible numbers increases by $1$. What number is on the back of the card showing $8$?

statementImages.alt: Three cards showing the numbers 3, 7, and 8.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: If the mean increases by $1$, then the total increases by $3$. So each flipped card reveals a number that is $3$ larger than the number that was showing. Thus the back of the $8$ is $8+3=11$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 11
statement: In the diagram, $ABCD$ is a rectangle and $\triangle ACE$ is right-angled at $C$. If $\angle BAC=42^\circ$, what is $\angle DCE$?

statementImages.alt: Rectangle ABCD with right triangle ACE attached at C.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Since $ABCD$ is a rectangle, $\angle ABC=90^\circ$. So in $\triangle ABC$, $$\angle ACB=180^\circ-90^\circ-42^\circ=48^\circ.$$ Because $\angle BCD=90^\circ$, we get $$\angle ACD=90^\circ-48^\circ=42^\circ.$$ Also $\angle ACE=90^\circ$, so $$\angle DCE=90^\circ-42^\circ=48^\circ.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 12
statement: Max was given $b$ books. In March he read one third of them. In April he read $5$ more books. He then had $7$ books left unread. What is $b$?
solution: After March, Max had $\frac{2}{3}b$ books left. After April, he had $$\frac{2}{3}b-5=7.$$ So $$\frac{2}{3}b=12,$$ and therefore $$b=18.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 13
statement: A circle of radius $K$ has area $15\pi$. What is the area of a circle of radius $2K$?
solution: If the radius doubles, the area is multiplied by $4$. So the new area is $$4\cdot 15\pi=60\pi.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 14
statement: If $$\frac{2m-n}{n}=\frac{3}{5},$$ find the value of $$\frac{m}{n}.$$
solution: From $$\frac{2m-n}{n}=\frac{3}{5},$$ we get $$5(2m-n)=3n.$$ So $10m-5n=3n$, hence $10m=8n$, and therefore $$\frac{m}{n}=\frac{8}{10}=\frac{4}{5}.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 15
statement: Three identical semicircles have diameters $PQ$, $RS$, and $TU$ placed along the same line, as shown. If $PR=SU=8$ and $QT=5$, what is the length of $PQ$?

statementImages.alt: Three identical semicircles with diameters PQ, RS, and TU on the same line.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Let the common diameter be $x$. Then $$PU=PQ+QT+TU=x+5+x=2x+5.$$ Also, $$PU=PR+RS+SU=8+x+8=x+16.$$ So $$2x+5=x+16,$$ which gives $x=11$. Thus $PQ=11$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 16
statement: Two boats leave the same dock. The Luna travels northwest at $8$ km/h for $1$ hour and stops. The Tuna travels northeast at $8$ km/h for $1$ hour, then due north for $90$ minutes at $8$ km/h and stops. To the nearest kilometre, what is the distance between the boats?
solution: After the first hour, each boat is $8$ km from the dock. The Tuna then goes $12$ km farther north. If $L$ and $T$ are the stopping points, then $$LT^2=8^2+8^2+12^2=64+64+144=272.$$ So $$LT=\sqrt{272}\approx 16.49,$$ which rounds to $16$ km.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 17
statement: Let $s$ be the sum of the digits of a positive two-digit integer $n$. Which of the following can be a value of $n-s$?
solution: If $n$ has tens digit $x$ and ones digit $y$, then $$n=10x+y,\qquad s=x+y.$$ So $$n-s=(10x+y)-(x+y)=9x,$$ which must be a positive multiple of $9$. Among the choices, only $54$ works.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 18
statement: A grasshopper starts at $(0,0)$ and makes exactly $6$ unit jumps, each either up, down, left, or right. It can end at $N$ different points. What is $N$?
solution: After $6$ moves, the parity of $x+y$ must be even. Also the distance condition gives $|x|+|y|\le 6$. The possible values of $|x|+|y|$ are $0,2,4,6$. For $|x|+|y|=6$, there are $24$ points. For $|x|+|y|=4$, there are $16$ points. For $|x|+|y|=2$, there are $8$ points. For $|x|+|y|=0$, there is $1$ point. So $$N=24+16+8+1=49.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 19
statement: Amira rolls a standard six-sided die exactly six times. The mean of her first three rolls is $3$. Her last three rolls are $a,b,c$. How many ordered triples $(a,b,c)$ make the mean of all six rolls an integer?
solution: The first three rolls sum to $9$. So the total mean is an integer exactly when $$\frac{9+a+b+c}{6}$$ is an integer, that is, when $9+a+b+c$ is a multiple of $6$. Since $a,b,c\in\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, the possible values of $a+b+c$ are from $3$ to $18$. We need $$a+b+c=3,9,15.$$ These contribute $1$, $25$, and $10$ ordered triples respectively, for a total of $$1+25+10=36.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 20
statement: All $5$-letter strings using only $A,B,C,D,E$ are listed in alphabetical order from $AAAAA$ to $EEEEE$. How many times does the letter $B$ occur in the $2026$th string?
solution: There are $5^5=3125$ strings. Each first letter appears in blocks of $5^4=625$, so the $2026$th string starts with $D$. Within the $D$-block, each second letter appears in blocks of $5^3=125$, so it starts with $DB$. Within the $DB$-block, each third letter appears in blocks of $25$, and the $2026$th string is the first string after $DBAEE$, namely $DBBAA$. That string contains exactly $2$ B's.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 21
statement: Four circles of radius $8$ are arranged so that each touches two others, and their centres are the vertices of a square. To the nearest integer, find the area of the shaded region enclosed by the four circles.

statementImages.alt: Four equal circles arranged in a square pattern with a shaded central region.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: The centres form a $16\times 16$ square. Inside that square, the circles cut out four quarter-circles of radius $8$, whose total area equals one full circle of radius $8$. So the shaded area is $$16\cdot 16-\pi(8)^2=256-64\pi\approx 54.94.$$ Rounded to the nearest integer, this is $55$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 22
statement: Of $100$ movie-watchers, $47$ bought popcorn, more than $40$ bought a drink, $x$ bought both, and $2x$ bought neither. If $x>0$, what is the largest possible value of $x$?
solution: Let $y$ be the number who bought a drink. Then $100=(47-x)+(y-x)+x+2x=47+y+x$, so $y=53-x$. Since more than $40$ bought a drink, we need $53-x>40$, so $x<13$. Thus the largest possible integer value of $x$ is $12$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 23
statement: For distinct positive integers $p,q,r$, the four numbers $$2r-21,\quad 3r-1,\quad r+12,\quad 3r-17$$ are equal to $p,2p,q,2q$ in some order. Find the sum of all possible values of $r$.
solution: Since the four given numbers are exactly $p,2p,q,2q$ in some order, their sum is $$3p+3q.$$ So $$(2r-21)+(3r-1)+(r+12)+(3r-17)=3p+3q,$$ which simplifies to $$9r-27=3p+3q,$$ hence $$p+q=3r-9.$$ Now try the possible assignments of two of the four expressions to be $p$ and $q$. The only valid cases are: $$p=3r-17,\quad 2p=2r-21 \Rightarrow 6r-34=2r-21 \Rightarrow r=41,$$ and $$p=2r-21,\quad 2p=3r-17 \Rightarrow 4r-42=3r-17 \Rightarrow r=25.$$ Thus the required sum is $$41+25=66.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 24
statement: In the shown $6\times 6\times 6$ cube, points $C$ and $F$ are vertices, and $A,B,D,E$ are midpoints of their edges. Lines $AF$ and $EC$ meet at $G$, and $BF$ and $DC$ meet at $H$. Pieces are sliced off by the planes $ABF$ and $EGHD$, and the same process is repeated on each of the four vertical edges. If the volume of the remaining solid is $V$, what are the rightmost two digits of $V$?

statementImages.alt: 6 by 6 by 6 cube with edge midpoints A, B, D, E and interior intersections G and H.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Let $$W=[EFDC],$$ the volume of pyramid $EFDC$. Since $EF=DF=3$ and $CF=6$, $$W=\frac{1}{3}\cdot \frac{1}{2}\cdot 3\cdot 3\cdot 6=9.$$ The inner pyramid removed from this pyramid is similar in each linear dimension by a factor of $\frac12$, so its volume is $\frac18W$. Thus the remaining sliced piece from one corner has volume $$W-\frac18W=\frac78W.$$ Two such pieces are removed from each vertical-edge section, so each section loses $$2\cdot \frac78W=\frac74W.$$ There are $4$ congruent sections, so the total removed volume is $$4\cdot \frac74W=7W=63.$$ The whole cube has volume $$6^3=216,$$ so the remaining volume is $$216-63=153.$$ The rightmost two digits are $53$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 25
statement: The integers from $1$ to $2k$ are written in order. Arthur erases $k$ consecutive integers. The remaining integers have sum $819$. Find the sum of all possible values of $k$.
solution: The sum of $1+2+\cdots+2k$ is $$\frac{2k(2k+1)}{2}=2k^2+k.$$ Suppose the erased block starts at $m$ and has length $k$. Then its sum is $$m+(m+1)+\cdots+(m+k-1)=km+\frac{(k-1)k}{2}.$$ So the remaining sum is $$2k^2+k-\left(km+\frac{(k-1)k}{2}\right)=819.$$ This simplifies to $$k(3k-2m+3)=1638.$$ Checking divisor pairs of $1638$ that can occur in this form gives exactly two valid values: $$k=26\quad\text{and}\quad k=39.$$ Their sum is $$26+39=65.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 1
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 2
statement: Which choice is closest to $\sqrt{37}$?
solution: Since $6^2=36$ and $7^2=49$, $\sqrt{37}$ is just a little more than $6$, so the closest choice is $6$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 3
solution: The left side is $8$, and $8=2^3$, so $x=3$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 4
statement: If $2\frac{3}{4}$, $2.3$, $2\frac{1}{4}$, $2.45$, and $2\frac{9}{10}$ are arranged from least to greatest, which one is in the middle?
solution: These numbers are $2.25$, $2.3$, $2.45$, $2.75$, and $2.9$. The middle number is $2.45$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 5
statement: In the cube sketch, $9$ edges are visible. How many edges are hidden?

statementImages.alt: Cube sketch for Pascal 2026 Problem 5.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: A cube has $12$ edges in total. If $9$ are visible, then $12-9=3$ are not visible.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 6
statement: Five routes from $A$ to $B$ are shown. Which route is the shortest?

statementImages.alt: Five gridded paths from A to B labelled Figure 1 through Figure 5.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: The shortest distance between two points is a straight line. Figure 4 is the only route that goes straight from $A$ to $B$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 7
statement: A train has one engine of length $20$ m and $n$ freight cars, each $15$ m long. If the total length is $140$ m, what is $n$?
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 8
statement: Which integer leaves the same remainder when divided by $3$ and by $4$?
solution: Check the choices. Only $13$ gives remainder $1$ when divided by $3$ and also remainder $1$ when divided by $4$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 9
statement: In the diagram, $\triangle PQT$ is equilateral. Also, $\triangle QST$ and $\triangle QRS$ are right triangles, and $QR=RS$. If $\angle STP=120^\circ$, what is $\angle PQR$?

statementImages.alt: Diagram with equilateral triangle PQT and right triangles QST and QRS.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: In the equilateral triangle, $\angle PQT=60^\circ$. Since $\angle QTP=60^\circ$ and $\angle STP=120^\circ$, we get $\angle STQ=60^\circ$. Then $\triangle QST$ is a $30^\circ\!-\!60^\circ\!-\!90^\circ$ triangle, so $\angle SQT=30^\circ$. Also, $\triangle QRS$ is isosceles right, so $\angle SQR=45^\circ$. Therefore $\angle PQR=60^\circ+30^\circ+45^\circ=135^\circ$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 10
statement: Six different $4\times 4$ squares have some region shaded. How many of them have exactly $25\%$ of their area shaded?

statementImages.alt: Six shaded 4 by 4 squares.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Each square has area $16$, so $25\%$ means shaded area $4$. Counting shaded area in square units gives $4,4,8,5,7,4$. Exactly three of the six squares have shaded area $4$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 11
statement: Each rectangle contains the sum of the two rectangles directly below it. What is the value of $y$?

statementImages.alt: Number pyramid with bottom row 9, 2, blank, 1, 3.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: The left rectangle in the second row is $9+2=11$. Since the $18$ above it equals $11+$ (middle second-row rectangle), that middle rectangle is $7$. Also, $x=1+3=4$, so $y=7+4=11$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 12
statement: Point $P(2,3)$ is reflected across the $x$-axis and then shifted $3$ units left. What point is reached?

statementImages.alt: Coordinate grid with point P at (2, 3).
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Reflecting across the $x$-axis sends $(2,3)$ to $(2,-3)$. Moving left $3$ units then gives $(-1,-3)$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 13
statement: Max started with $b$ books. He read one third of them in March, then read $5$ more books in April, and had $7$ books left. What is $b$?
solution: After March and April, Max has $b-\frac{b}{3}-5=7$. So $\frac{2b}{3}=12$, which gives $b=18$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 14
statement: Square $WXYZ$ has side length $15$. Point $V$ is on $WX$ and $VZ=17$. What is the area of trapezoid $VXYZ$?

statementImages.alt: Square WXYZ with point V on WX and segment VZ.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Let $WV=t$. Then $\triangle WVZ$ is right with legs $t$ and $15$, and hypotenuse $17$. So $t^2+15^2=17^2$, giving $t=8$. Thus $VX=15-8=7$. The trapezoid has parallel sides $7$ and $15$ and distance $15$ between them, so its area is $\frac{7+15}{2}\cdot 15=165$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 15
statement: Peter's car uses $10.2$ L per $100$ km. Mike's hybrid uses $6.6$ L per $100$ km. Fuel costs $\$1.40$ per litre. If each drives $200$ km, how much less does Mike spend?
solution: Over $200$ km, Peter uses $20.4$ L and Mike uses $13.2$ L, so Mike uses $7.2$ L less. At $\$1.40$ per litre, the savings are $7.2\times 1.40=\$10.08$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 16
statement: Points $P,Q,R,S$ lie on a segment in that order. If $PR=8$ and $QS=15$, what is the smallest possible integer value of $PS$?

statementImages.alt: Points P, Q, R, S on one line in that order.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Let $PQ=a$, $QR=b$, and $RS=c$, where $a,b,c>0$. Then $a+b=8$ and $b+c=15$, so $PS=a+b+c=23-b$. Because $a>0$, we have $b<8$, so $PS>15$. Values arbitrarily close to $15$ are possible, so the smallest possible integer value is $16$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 17
statement: Four of the five puzzle pieces can be rotated and fitted together, without overlap and without flipping, to make a square. Which piece is not used?

statementImages.alt: Five puzzle pieces labelled 1 through 5.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: The curved tab and curved notch pieces must be used so the curved edges disappear inside the square, so pieces $2$, $3$, and $5$ are needed. Piece $3$ has the only triangular tab, so piece $1$ is needed to match its triangular notch. That leaves piece $4$ unused.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 18
statement: The sum $17+18+\cdots+352$ is $61\,992$. What is the value of $20+21+\cdots+355$?
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 19
statement: The figure is made of thirty-four $1\times 1$ squares. Counting only squares formed by the grid lines, how many squares of all sizes appear in the figure?

statementImages.alt: Stacked grid figure made of thirty-four unit squares.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: There are $34$ squares of size $1\times 1$. There are $22$ squares of size $2\times 2$, $12$ of size $3\times 3$, and $4$ of size $4\times 4$. So the total is $34+22+12+4=72$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 20
statement: A bag has two quarters, two dimes, and two nickels. Two coins are chosen at random. What is the probability that their total value is at least $\$0.30$?
solution: There are $\binom{6}{2}=15$ equally likely pairs. The successful pairs are one quarter-quarter pair, four quarter-dime pairs, and four quarter-nickel pairs, for $1+4+4=9$ good pairs. Thus the probability is $\frac{9}{15}=\frac{3}{5}$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 21
statement: How many three-digit lock codes use distinct digits from $1$ to $9$, are in increasing order, and have third digit equal to the sum of the first two?
solution: If the first two digits are $a<b$, then the third digit is forced to be $a+b$. Since the digits are increasing, we just need $a+b\le 9$. Counting pairs gives $7$ choices for $a=1$, $5$ for $a=2$, $3$ for $a=3$, and $1$ for $a=4$. This totals $7+5+3+1=16$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 22
statement: The five distinct integers $16$, $x$, $8$, $17$, and $11$ have the same mean and median. Find the sum of all possible values of $x$.
solution: The mean is $\frac{52+x}{5}$. Check where $x$ can lie in the ordered list. If $x<8$, the median is $11$, so $\frac{52+x}{5}=11$ gives $x=3$. If $11<x<16$, then the median is $x$, so $\frac{52+x}{5}=x$ gives $x=13$. If $x>17$, the median is $16$, so $\frac{52+x}{5}=16$ gives $x=28$. These are the only valid cases, and $3+13+28=44$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 23
statement: A square of side length $10$ has a semicircle drawn inward on each side. The overlapping parts form a shaded four-petal flower. What integer is closest to the area of the shaded region?

statementImages.alt: Square with four overlapping semicircles forming a shaded four-petal flower.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: Each petal is made from two $90^\circ$ sectors of radius $5$, minus two right isosceles triangles with legs $5$. So one petal has area $2\left(\frac{25\pi}{4}-\frac{25}{2}\right)=\frac{25\pi}{2}-25$. The whole flower has area $4\left(\frac{25\pi}{2}-25\right)=50\pi-100\approx 57.08$, so the closest integer is $57$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 24
statement: Form a digit string by writing $1234$, then one $5$, then $1234$, then two $5$s, then $1234$, then three $5$s, and so on. If $S$ is the sum of the first $2026$ digits, what is the sum of the digits of $S$?
solution: The first $m$ blocks have total length $(m^2+9m)/2$. For $m=59$, this is $2006$, so the first $59$ blocks fit and the $60$th block contributes the next $20$ digits. The digit sum of the first $59$ blocks is $\sum_{k=1}^{59}(10+5k)=9440$. The next $20$ digits are $1234$ followed by sixteen $5$s, whose sum is $10+80=90$. Thus $S=9440+90=9530$, and the sum of its digits is $9+5+3+0=17$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Pascal 2026 Problem 25
statement: Start with an equilateral triangle of side length $6$. Remove smaller equilateral triangles with positive integer side lengths $a\le b\le c$ from its three corners, where $a+b<6$, $a+c<6$, and $b+c<6$. The remaining figure is a hexagon that can be tiled by exactly $n$ identical equilateral triangles of some positive integer side length. If $M$ is the sum of all possible values of $n$, what are the rightmost two digits of $M$?

statementImages.alt: Figure 1, Figure 2, and Figure 3 showing the equilateral triangle cuts and the resulting hexagon.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution: The possible triples are $(1,1,1)$, $(1,1,2)$, $(1,1,3)$, $(1,1,4)$, $(1,2,2)$, $(1,2,3)$, $(2,2,2)$, and $(2,2,3)$. For each triple, the hexagon area in unit equilateral triangles is $36-a^2-b^2-c^2$, giving $33,30,25,18,27,22,24,$ and $19$. These all occur by tiling with side-$1$ equilateral triangles. A larger tile side is possible only when every hexagon side length is even, which happens only for $(2,2,2)$; then the hexagon can also be tiled by $6$ side-$2$ equilateral triangles. So the possible values of $n$ are $33,30,25,18,27,22,24,19,$ and $6$, whose sum is $204$. The rightmost two digits are $04$.
credit: 2026 Pascal Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo
